interactions.summary: Jiang recaps the course's origin story: humans spread from Africa under climate pressure, early religion was animistic, and the Mother Goddess model made humans, animals, and plants part of one family.

He frames the week's problem as a historical reversal: if humans lived for most of history in a compassionate, egalitarian, artistic order, the lecture must explain how war, private property, and patriarchy appeared.

He introduces Proto-Indo-European by showing cognates such as father, mother, and two across European, Iranian, and Indian languages, then says linguists reconstructed a shared mother language and debated whether it spread from Anatolia or Ukraine.
He explains the linguistic method: words that survive in the reconstructed language reveal lifestyle. The vocabulary points to wheels, dairy, limited agriculture, and horses.
He says archaeology and recent DNA work have started confirming the hypotheses first generated from language.

speaker_notes.note: Continuous classroom lecture. The repeated 'does that make sense' and prompt questions are Jiang's teaching cadence, not independent public questions.

claims.claim: Jiang presents early human religion as animistic: humans, animals, and plants all come from one Mother Goddess source, which grounds an egalitarian and compassionate social order.
claims.temporal_scope: Course model as stated in the 2024-09-10 lecture

claims.claim: The lecture's central historical problem is how war, private property, and patriarchy emerged after a long period Jiang characterizes as mostly egalitarian, peaceful, and artistic.
Jiang says Proto-Indo-European is a reconstructed mother language behind European, Iranian, and Indian languages, supported by cognates such as father, mother, and two.
claims.temporal_scope: Historical-linguistic evidence as presented in the lecture

claims.claim: The reconstructed vocabulary lets Jiang infer four traits of the Proto-Indo-Europeans: wheels, dairy consumption, relatively few agricultural terms, and horse domestication.
Jiang says archaeology and DNA evidence have confirmed many hypotheses that linguists first generated from Proto-Indo-European vocabulary.
claims.temporal_scope: Assessment in the 2024-09-10 lecture, referring especially to recent genetic work

signature_moments.moment: The Mother Goddess makes the world one kinship system: humans, animals, and plants all come from one source.
signature_moments.source_phrase: we all come from one source, the Mother Goddess
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture's first moral premise; peace and equality are not policy choices but consequences of a sacred family model.

signature_moments.moment: The lecture turns human history into a fall: from Mother Goddess egalitarianism to war, private property, and patriarchy.
signature_moments.source_phrase: how is it that today we have war, private property, and patriarchy
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the episode its Paradise Lost structure and makes Old Europe the lost height before conquest.

signature_moments.moment: Language becomes an archaeological instrument: a list of words turns into a picture of diet, technology, animals, and movement.
signature_moments.source_phrase: what these words tell us is the culture and lifestyle
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is Jiang's method for moving from words to civilization before the lecture moves to DNA and burial evidence.

signature_moments.moment: Horse domestication is framed as overcoming panic: horses survive by running away, and civilization begins when that excitability is mastered.
signature_moments.source_phrase: If you see someone coming, you run away. This is what we call excitability.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The phrase gives a bodily image to horse domestication and sets up the pastoralist power that will later destroy Old Europe.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's name for the animistic source of life that makes humans, animals, and plants one sacred family.
Maria Gimbutas's term, as Jiang presents it, for the high point of the pre-conquest Mother Goddess civilization in Europe.
A reconstructed lost mother language behind many European, Iranian, and Indian languages.
Jiang's description of the horse's tendency to flee danger, making domestication difficult.

chronology_notes.note: This episode states the premise for a course sequence: today's class treats Old Europe's height, while later/next classes explain conquest and transformation.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR renders several site names phonetically; semantic extraction treats them as references to Ice Age paintings, Gobekli Tepe, and Catalhoyuk without changing the transcript.
Audience prompts such as 'what patterns do you see' and 'why is this strange' appear in Jiang's lecture flow; no separate substantive student question is recoverable in this packet.
